TextENGNew: ...I went home... and washed off the unpleasant sweat of the day in the shower.
Normally, a shower was all it took to reset everything—to wash away all the bad things that had happened.
...But not today.
When I stepped out of the shower, I found fresh underwear placed neatly in the laundry basket.
...I never usually gave it a second thought, but today this small kindness from my mother made me genuinely happy.
...Yet at the same time, it reminded me of Satoko's suffering.
At the very moment I was savoring this modest tenderness, Satoko might have been getting hurt by her uncle's heartless words.
I went upstairs and shut myself in my room.
...I sat down at my desk... and folded my arms.
The subject on my mind, of course... was Satoko.
...Stubborn as she was, Satoko would refuse to acknowledge the problem... and try to endure it all in silence.
...In Satoko's mind, this was an act of atonement... toward Satoshi, who had always shielded her and then disappeared.
...As long as Satoko herself believed that... the situation would be far from simple.
But just a while ago, I had made a clear declaration in front of Coach.
...If I believed Satoko was in danger... I would act on my own judgment.
In the end, I was still just taking a wait-and-see approach too, though...
...Even so, I felt I had drawn a clearer line than my friends or Coach had.
...............But hold on, Keiichi Maebara.
...Would just reporting it really be enough to solve the problem...?
...What if... they ended up taking the same wait-and-see approach as last year...?
Leaving everything in the hands of adults was dangerous. ...On top of that... I had to find a way to ensure Satoko's safety...
I scratched furiously at my head, then tilted it back, desperate for even a shred of composure.
...............The shouting match with my friends earlier today came flooding back.
I was ashamed that I hadn't realized it until Rena pointed it out.
...That's right.
...My house was big.
...Compared to the thatched-roof houses scattered around Hinamizawa... it was much bigger.
We actually had rooms to spare.
...I'd never thought of us as affluent... but I'd never thought of us as poor, either.
...I just never admitted it because I didn't want to seem arrogant...
...But maybe... my family really was well-off.
...We had plenty of rooms we could lend to Satoko.
...The guest rooms were nothing but empty space, aside from the occasional work associate of my dad's staying over.
...And some of the rooms my dad used for storage could easily become proper rooms if we just tidied them up.
...As for food expenses... that might be a more serious issue than a kid like me could appreciate.
Lunch would be manageable, at least.
...All of us friends would just need to pack a little extra in our lunches from now on.
We were always sharing food with each other anyway, so it'd work out.
...But breakfast and dinner... those would have to depend on Mom.
...Convincing her to do more than just lend a room would be necessary.@k(Of course, I didn't think convincing her even of that would be easy...)
How much did it actually cost... to feed one person...?
Would it be... tens of thousands of yen a month...?@k If I... covered that myself, they couldn't complain, right?
...I only had maybe ten or twenty thousand yen in my piggy bank, but it was something.

I actually had more saved up from New Year's money and such... but my parents had confiscated all of that and put it in a fixed-term deposit.@k...If I could access that, it should amount to a decent sum.
If I was going this far... I should ask Mion and Rena and the others to share some of the burden.
Of course, I wouldn't count on it.
...Rena had gotten angry at me, hadn't she?@kFor always trying to push responsibility onto others.
...I would ask for help.
But... fundamentally, I would be the one saving her.
I would see it through...!
Oh, but... it wasn't just about food expenses.
...There were so many essential things you couldn't go without when actually living somewhere—the bath, laundry, and more.
...My mother was the fastidious type, ruthlessly strict about any kind of waste—and for once, that was a problem.
...She might even bring up... the cost of the detergent Satoko would use to wash her own clothes.
...Food expenses alone wouldn't cut it.
I... needed more money.

(Hey, hey, Keiichi Maebara—when did this turn into 'money solves everything'?!@k Even if you had the money... you'd need your parents' permission in the first place!@k Taking in a girl for an extended period of time...!)
What on earth would I say to persuade them...?
...If you had even an ounce of sense, Keiichi Maebara... you'd realize right away that it's impossible.
Even if they listened to me seriously, they'd just tell me to contact the police.
And even if I managed to win their sympathy, the question would become: why should the Maebara family bear the entire burden?
That's how it would go.
...Exactly.
...It was painfully sad and frustrating, but... no matter how strong my resolve was...
...my resolve alone... couldn't save anyone.
"............Are kids... really this powerless...?"
...I was frustrated.
...I'd believed my feelings were second to none.@k...I'd even thought they rivaled Coach's.@k...And yet...
Just then, two knocks came at my door, and Mom poked her head in.
"You're still up?@k@vS03/00/142700002. I've been calling you down for dinner for a while now."
"...Oh... yeah, I'll be right there..."
I know I shouldn't say this as someone who still relies on his parents for everything, but...
...all I had to do was sit there, and food would just appear before me.
...It had always felt so natural that I'd shamelessly thought of it as the obligation of the parents who brought me into this world.
...But when I realized that this 'natural' thing was actually a privilege...
...I understood just how difficult it was to extend that privilege to someone else...
Even the unremarkable, plain dinner in front of me held a deeper meaning tonight.
Right now, meals for three were laid out on the table—Dad, Mom, and me.
...How hard would it be... to add just one more place setting...?
...........Think, Keiichi Maebara.
...If making food for four was too hard... then I just needed to figure out how four people could eat food made for three.
...By flipping my perspective... an unexpectedly bold plan began to take shape...
That's it.
...I didn't need my parents' permission.@k...She could just live here in secret.

I'd gotten in trouble for it before, but... if I remembered right, you could get in and out of my room from outside by climbing through the window and making your way across the first-floor roof or down the rain gutter.
Satoko was more athletic than me, so she'd manage it even more easily.
...I hadn't considered it until now, but having her live here secretly was actually essential.
It would be reassuring to have my parents' cooperation, but... there's that saying about having to fool your allies first to fool your enemies...
...All right.
...Trying to persuade my parents was unrealistic... so I'd explore the option of hiding her—a covert living arrangement.
When I was home, she'd just need to keep quiet in my room upstairs.
...The problem was the daytime.
If she was hiding from her uncle, she shouldn't go to school.

...She'd be lonely, but it would be better if she didn't attend.@k(I could easily teach her the material from her grade level.@k...In fact, at school I spend more time helping the younger kids study than doing my own work!)
...During the day, I'd be at school, so I wouldn't be home.
...Now that I was old enough, my parents respected my privacy and wouldn't barge into my room while I was out.@k(...I think.)@k...If she stayed hidden in my room... it should be fine... right...?
If my parents did come up, the second-floor location meant she'd hear their footsteps on the stairs in advance.
And there'd be a brief delay before they reached the top (...maybe a few seconds, I estimated), during which she could hide in the closet...
Wait, wait, Keiichi!@kSomething doesn't add up...!!
If Satoko can't go to school in the first place, what's she going to do about lunch?!
...Calm down, me!@kI could just leave my own lunch behind for her.
I'd go to school without a lunch and have everyone share a bit of theirs with me.
...Okay... that works.@k...Any more contradictions or blind spots?
...Right—breakfast and dinner.
...She could probably go without breakfast somehow.
...On Sundays when I sleep through the morning, two meals a day is perfectly fine.
For dinner... first, I'd pretend my appetite had grown and ask for larger portions.
...And then, I'd somehow find a way to pass part of my meal to Satoko...!
...As a test...
...I stood up from my seat, carrying my plate of grilled fish.
"What's wrong, Keiichi?@k@vS03/00/142700004. Sit down and eat your meal properly."
"Oh, um.@k@vS03/01/140100238. ...I just wanted a change of scenery, you know?@k@vS03/01/140100239. ...Would it be okay if I ate in my room?"
changeReason: Old says 'I'm kind of not in a good mood'; new says 'I just wanted a change of scenery.' The Japanese 気分が変えたくなって means 'wanted to change my mood/atmosphere,' making the new translation more accurate.

TextENGNew: "...You'll drop crumbs everywhere and make a mess.@k@vS03/00/142600002. Eat your meals at the table."
...Oh, come on...
I just tried to walk off with one measly plate of fish... and they both shut me down?!
Sneaking food out from under my parents' noses... was impossible in practice.
...But surely... it was worth thinking about.
...There had to be some kind of trick—some method using misdirection or blind spots—to pull it off...
(Even if I can't figure it out today, I might come up with something tomorrow...!)
My appetite was fading anyway, so I finished eating early and returned to my room.
...Back in my room... I decided to put myself in Satoko's shoes.
...For instance, suppose my parents were coming up the stairs right now. Quick—hide...!
I opened the closet.
...Rattle, rattle.@k...It didn't open quietly.
Come on... didn't this thing usually slide open nice and smooth?!
Why was it suddenly sticking now?!@kWas this a defective house?!@kWas it falling apart already?!@kDid the rails just need wax?!@kShould I slather them in lubricant?!
...The sound itself might have been faint... but it was unsettling to think my parents might notice it when coming up the stairs.
...But this was the kind of problem I could solve.
I just needed to tinker with it a bit so it wouldn't make noise anymore.
For now... let's say I managed to hide successfully, and I'll try staying inside the closet.
...I basically never put my futon away—it was always out on the floor.
I couldn't think of any reason my parents would open my closet.
...But there still might be some occasion where they would.
I might need to rig up some kind of camouflage so she wouldn't be spotted even if they did open it.
But the more elaborate the setup... the less time Satoko would have to actually hide, and the greater the chance they'd hear something...
...Just then... I suddenly needed to use the bathroom.
...The bathroom...?!
Something so utterly obvious... turned out to be a fatal flaw...
The only toilet was on the first floor.
There was absolutely no way she could use it without someone in the house noticing.
...A bath was a different story.
She could use it when my family was out.
But a toilet was a completely different matter.
...If her stomach started acting up while my parents were milling around downstairs... there'd be nothing she could do...!
...Before I knew it... I'd been digging my nails into my scalp with both hands.
I sat with my knees drawn up in the pitch-dark closet.@k...I buried my face against my knees and clawed at my head.
...The more I thought, the more contradictions appeared.
...The more I thought, the more the plan fell apart...@kThe more I agonized... the more I was forced to confront my own powerlessness.
My spine had started to ache from being crammed in such a tight space for so long.
But if I sheltered Satoko here, this was the pain she would have to endure.
...Living in a place this dark, this cramped... this suffocating... indefinitely.
Even so, it would still be far better than living under her uncle's roof... or at least, I wanted to believe that.
...It became hard to breathe... and I finally gave in, crawling out of the closet.
I glanced at the clock... and to my shock, it was already 3:30 a.m.
...Time had passed with unbelievable speed.
The moment I acknowledged that, a crushing wave of drowsiness hit me, as if it had been waiting for me to notice the hour.
I couldn't resist its pull... and collapsed facedown onto my futon.
...Damn it. I can't just fall asleep like this.
...If I wasted time here, I'd be no different from Mion and Coach and the rest who had chosen to wait and see.
I had to... spend every minute, every second, worrying and thinking harder than anyone else... to find a way to save Satoko...
...........Isn't there a better way?
...Isn't there a better way?
...That single question, muttered to myself over and over, spiraled round and round... gradually swallowing my consciousness whole............
In my last moment of awareness, I thought:
...What I'd come up with tonight was riddled with blind spots... but the underlying idea wasn't wrong at all.
...Tomorrow, I would propose this bold plan to the others.
...Mion might be able to contribute something, and Rena, with her unexpectedly sharp mind, might offer a good suggestion.
...And above all else...
...we needed to reaffirm our commitment... to work together and rescue Satoko...
...........................I felt pathetic for surrendering to sleep.
...I'm sorry... Satoko...
